question: Tell me about yourself.

stories.title: Grambling CS foundation
stories.situation: I am Emmanuel Acheampong, pursuing a B.S. in Computer Science at Grambling State University (May 2027) with Dean’s List honors (‘24, ‘25), a Presidential Scholarship, and a Vanda African Math Olympiad (2018) gold—coursework spans OOP, software engineering, DSA, and OS.
stories.task: Translate academic rigor into production-style engineering habits.
stories.action: I paired theory with internships (Geaux, Hubtel) and large projects (MedDiagnose, VisualizeMyAlgorithm, Mini-Compiler) so algorithms and systems concepts show up in measurable shipping work.
stories.result: That combination is why I default to traceability, tests, and metrics when I build.

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: At Geaux Network (Associate SWE Intern, May–Aug 2025, remote), engineering and content both needed auditability for edits to podcasts and media metadata, but events lived in multiple backend sources with inconsistent schemas.
stories.task: Ship a Change History View in TypeScript/SCSS that traced content and media changes without boiling the ocean on day one.
stories.action: I aligned engineering and content in one working session, ranked event sources by traceability value, and implemented a phased UI that surfaced the highest-signal protobuf-backed events first; paired that with tighter integration tests on the read path.
stories.result: We improved end-to-end traceability of edits across the platform and kept scope controlled so releases stayed predictable.

stories.title: Kafka/MQTT/Postgres pipeline
stories.situation: At Hubtel (SWE Intern, Jun–Sep 2023, Ghana), transaction-heavy services generated high-volume system events; downtime and delayed detection were directly tied to revenue and trust.
stories.task: Build observability that shortened mean time to detect issues without destabilizing payment workflows.
stories.action: I helped build a scalable real-time pipeline using Apache Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL, traced failures end-to-end from broker to sink, and validated assumptions with integration tests and metrics.
stories.result: Downtime detection time improved by about 40% and platform availability for payment flows got materially better.

question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with your manager.

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation

stories.task: Ship a Change History View in TypeScript/SCSS that traced content and media changes without boiling the ocean on day one.
stories.action: I aligned engineering and content in one working session, ranked event sources by traceability value, and implemented a phased UI that surfaced the highest-signal protobuf-backed events first; paired that with tighter integration tests on the read path.
stories.result: We improved end-to-end traceability of edits across the platform and kept scope controlled so releases stayed predictable.

stories.title: Spring Boot test expansion
stories.situation: Payment and financial workflows needed higher confidence than a thin layer of unit tests; failures were often integration-shaped (DB, messaging, HTTP).
stories.task: Increase reliability of backend services supporting money movement.
stories.action: I wrote 20+ automated unit and integration tests with Spring Boot, JUnit, and Postman collections, focusing on transaction boundaries and failure modes—not just coverage numbers.
stories.result: Reliability improved on the order of 80% in the areas we targeted, and regressions became easier to catch pre-release.

stories.title: Cross-officer conflict
stories.situation: Officers split on whether to prioritize hackathons, corporate sessions, or K-12 outreach—each had vocal advocates.
stories.task: Convert debate into a quarterly plan with measurable outcomes.
stories.action: I quantified member demand (surveys + attendance), tied sponsor commitments to specific events, and sequenced initiatives so wins compounded.
stories.result: Execution sped up and internal friction dropped because priorities were evidence-backed.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: ColorStack (member, Sep 2024–present) runs high-signal hackathons with global competition; Winter 2024 had ~1,000 participants.
stories.task: Ship a compelling project under clock pressure with a clear demo.
stories.action: Our team scoped to an achievable MVP, divided frontend/backend ownership, and rehearsed the demo path to avoid live-integration surprises.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024—validation that I can execute under pressure with distributed teammates.

question: Tell me about a time you failed. How did you deal with the situation?

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Blood-sample analytics used a Random Forest model for stroke-risk signals; clinicians cared about calibration, failure modes, and when to ignore the score.
stories.task: Make ML assistive without pretending it replaced judgment.
stories.action: I translated model inputs/outputs into clinician language, documented uncertainty, and wired the UI so human decision points stayed explicit.
stories.result: We preserved trust while still delivering automation where it mattered—routing, alerts, and eligibility prep.

stories.title: Correctness first
stories.situation: Early versions prioritized features; subtle parser bugs cascaded into bad IR.
stories.task: Stabilize the pipeline before adding optimizations.
stories.action: I tightened grammar handling, added regression tests around edge cases, then layered optimizations once baselines were green.
stories.result: The compiler became teachable and trustworthy enough for others to extend.

stories.title: gRPC service layer + tests
stories.situation: The podcast stack needed a maintainable API surface: internal services were moving toward Java services exposing gRPC with Protocol Buffer contracts, and accuracy regressions were costly for ingestion and distribution.
stories.task: Raise data fidelity for services feeding podcast ingestion and distribution to 10,000+ monthly listeners while keeping contracts testable.
stories.action: I implemented and extended the service layer in Java/gRPC with protobuf definitions, added end-to-end coverage across the RPC boundary, and validated behavior against real ingestion workflows—not just happy-path unit tests.
stories.result: We roughly doubled measured data accuracy on the ingestion path and made downstream consumers safer to evolve.

question: Describe a time when you led a team. What was the outcome?

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: As NSBE Gen-Sec (Jan 2025–present, Grambling), the chapter needed momentum after pandemic-era engagement drops; CS majors wanted technical community, not just social events.
stories.task: Rebuild membership energy and secure sustainable sponsor relationships.
stories.action: I operationalized outreach, aligned officers on a member-first roadmap, and packaged the chapter for sponsors with clear talent and impact metrics.
stories.result: We grew to 300+ members and secured ~$3K in technical partnerships.

stories.title: MedDiagnose stack + pilot
stories.situation: MedDiagnose AI (Python, TypeScript, FastAPI, TensorFlow, React) targeted stroke workflows: real-time monitoring, NIHSS scoring, RBAC, and automated tPA eligibility checks to cut diagnostic delay.
stories.task: Ship a hospital-defensible pilot in Louisiana, not a demo that overclaimed ML performance.
stories.action: I worked across FastAPI services, React clinical UI, and model integration—framing the Random Forest risk layer honestly at ~45% accuracy while keeping the workflow valuable for neurologists.
stories.result: A Louisiana hospital pilot strengthened early detection and cut time-to-treatment by ~35%, improving care coordination outcomes.

stories.title: VisualizeMyAlgorithm scale
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm (React.js) is an interactive algorithm visualization platform—pathfinding, sorting, and AI-style simulations—used by 1,000+ learners.
stories.task: Make heavy visualizations feel instant and understandable, not gimmicky.
stories.action: I built modular visualization components (Dijkstra, A*, Minimax, neural-net demos), dynamic state management, and a live rendering pipeline for step-by-step execution.
stories.result: Learners reported grasping concepts faster—about 40% faster in informal timing—and the platform sustained large concurrent sessions without frame collapse.

stories.result: That reduced privilege ambiguity, improved audit posture, and made cross-team collaboration less error-prone.

question: Describe a time you received tough or critical feedback.

stories.title: Service-layer maintainability
stories.situation: Early gRPC/protobuf service code I shipped at Geaux was correct in tests but hard for teammates to extend—naming and module boundaries obscured contract ownership.
stories.task: Absorb critique without defending ego; raise code quality for the team’s velocity.
stories.action: I refactored protobuf packaging, added contract tests at the RPC boundary, and documented error semantics so consumers could reason about failures.
stories.result: Review friction dropped and subsequent features shipped faster with fewer integration surprises.

stories.title: ML calibration reality check
stories.situation: Mentors pushed back that marketing the stroke RF model by raw accuracy (~45%) misled non-technical readers.
stories.task: Reframe metrics responsibly.
stories.action: I shifted reporting to calibration plots, confusion-aware language, and workflow KPIs (time-to-treatment) instead of headline accuracy.
stories.result: Stakeholders trusted the dashboard more; clinicians engaged more deeply.

stories.title: Compiler scope creep
stories.situation: On Mini-Compiler, a reviewer said I optimized before stabilizing parse errors—classic premature optimization.
stories.task: Reorder the roadmap to correctness-first.
stories.action: I parked IR passes until lexer/parser tests covered edge tokens; then reintroduced optimizations with benchmarks.
stories.result: Stability improved and the critique became moot.

question: Describe a time when you had to give someone difficult feedback. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Peer tests at Hubtel
stories.situation: A teammate wanted to merge a Spring service change with thin integration coverage around a payment edge case I had seen fail in staging.
stories.task: Raise risk without attacking them personally.
stories.action: I reproduced the failure with a minimal Postman sequence, shared logs, and suggested a focused JUnit + Testcontainers case; framed it as shared quality bar, not blame.
stories.result: They expanded tests; we merged with confidence.

stories.title: NSBE officer workload
stories.situation: An officer consistently over-promised event turnout, which strained sponsors and volunteers.
stories.task: Give candid feedback privately with data.
stories.action: I showed attendance trends vs. promises and proposed smaller committed events; focused on their strengths in marketing while tightening planning cadence.
stories.result: The next quarter hit targets without burning volunteers.

stories.title: Hackathon scope
stories.situation: During ColorStack Winter, a teammate wanted a live ML API dependency we could not stabilize in 12 hours.
stories.task: Push back with alternatives fast.
stories.action: I proposed a mocked inference boundary with recorded outputs for demo reliability and documented the tradeoff for judges.
stories.result: We shipped on time and still told an honest technical story.

stories.title: RBAC + a11y baseline
stories.situation: Inclusive design for me starts with permissions, keyboard paths, and screen-reader labels on critical flows—especially admin/creator splits.

stories.title: Full-stack reliability

stories.situation: The product goal was learning efficiency, not flashy graphics.

stories.action: I synchronized visual state with algorithm invariants (frontier sets, relaxed edges) so users could map code to motion.
stories.result: That reduced “magic” and improved retention for CS fundamentals.

question: Why are you interested in working at [company name]?

stories.title: Impact + systems
stories.situation: I gravitate to teams where code ties to observable outcomes—latency, availability, accuracy, or learning velocity—not vanity features.

stories.result: I want that slope to continue—deeper ownership on services and cross-functional delivery.

stories.action: Keep investing in estimation and cross-team communication.
stories.result: Next level is owning larger slices with clearer SLOs and mentoring others on test design.

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: As NSBE Gen-Sec (Jan 2025–present, Grambling), the chapter needed momentum after pandemic-era engagement drops; CS majors wanted technical community, not just social events.
stories.task: Rebuild membership energy and secure sustainable sponsor relationships.
stories.action: I operationalized outreach, aligned officers on a member-first roadmap, and packaged the chapter for sponsors with clear talent and impact metrics.
stories.result: We grew to 300+ members and secured ~$3K in technical partnerships.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: ColorStack (member, Sep 2024–present) runs high-signal hackathons with global competition; Winter 2024 had ~1,000 participants.
stories.task: Ship a compelling project under clock pressure with a clear demo.
stories.action: Our team scoped to an achievable MVP, divided frontend/backend ownership, and rehearsed the demo path to avoid live-integration surprises.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024—validation that I can execute under pressure with distributed teammates.

question: What is your biggest strength and area of growth?

stories.title: Strength: end-to-end debugging
stories.situation: I stay calm tracing failures across protobuf, Kafka/MQTT, and UI—evidence from Hubtel and Geaux.
stories.task: Growth: earlier escalation
stories.action: Sometimes I dig too deep before looping in leads; I am learning to time-box investigation and escalate with a crisp summary.
stories.result: Leads get signal sooner; I still own follow-through on fixes.

stories.title: Strength: responsible ML framing
stories.situation: MedDiagnose taught me to pair metrics with clinical workflow value.
stories.task: Growth: formal MLOps
stories.action: I want deeper experience with monitoring drift and automated retraining in production.
stories.result: I am closing that gap with coursework side projects and reading production ML ops playbooks.

stories.title: Strength: community leadership
stories.situation: NSBE numbers and partnerships show execution.
stories.task: Growth: delegation at scale
stories.action: Continue building leaders under me, not just running events.
stories.result: Chapter initiatives now survive without me in the critical path.

question: What aspects of your work are most often criticized?

stories.title: Over-engineering risk
stories.situation: I sometimes reach for robust abstractions early—reviewers push me to prove need with data first.
stories.task: Balance elegance and speed.
stories.action: I now default to simplest change that meets SLO, then refactor when patterns repeat.
stories.result: PRs land faster with fewer philosophical debates.

stories.title: Estimation optimism
stories.situation: Compiler and visualization projects taught me that visual polish hides integration cost.
stories.task: Pad estimates for unknowns.
stories.action: I break work into vertical slices with demos.
stories.result: Stakeholders get predictable milestones instead of surprise slips.

stories.title: Async updates
stories.situation: Remote work means I must over-communicate status—something mentors flagged.
stories.task: More frequent checkpoints.
stories.action: I post short daily/weekly deltas in standups and docs so blockers surface earlier.
stories.result: Managers stopped chasing me for status—it is already there.

question: Tell me about a time you needed information from someone who wasn't responsive.

stories.title: Blocked on proto review
stories.situation: A busy senior engineer delayed reviewing a protobuf change blocking my Geaux feature.
stories.task: Unblock without nagging.
stories.action: I sent a minimal diff summary, listed concrete questions, and offered two 15-minute slots; escalated with data on downstream blockers only after SLA slipped.
stories.result: Review landed; team respected the structure.

stories.title: Clinical feedback loop
stories.situation: Neurologists were slow to answer MedDiagnose UX questions during finals week.
stories.task: Respect their time.
stories.action: I batch questions, brought screenshots, and used async Loom walkthroughs.
stories.result: We got answers without burning relationship capital.

stories.title: Sponsor contact ghosting
stories.situation: For NSBE partnerships, a company contact went quiet while we needed budget confirmation before booking travel.
stories.task: Secure a yes/no without damaging the relationship.
stories.action: I used a polite escalation path—shorter email with two options (commit by date X or pause)—and CC’d our faculty advisor only when necessary.
stories.result: We got a decision in time to book at non-refundable rates or pivot to another sponsor.

question: What frustrates you?

stories.title: Opaque incidents
stories.situation: I get frustrated when postmortems skip root cause or action items—“fixed” without learning.
stories.task: Demand blameless depth.
stories.action: At Hubtel I pushed for timelines, contributing factors, and preventive tests—not just restarts.
stories.result: Repeat incidents dropped when we fixed upstream backpressure instead of blaming Postgres.

stories.title: Vanity metrics
stories.situation: Shipping features without SLOs or user impact frustrates me.
stories.task: Tie work to numbers.
stories.action: I anchor demos to latency, error budgets, or clinical KPIs—MedDiagnose’s 35% time-to-treatment improvement, not slide filler.
stories.result: Teams align faster when success is measurable.

stories.title: Gatekeeping
stories.situation: Communities that discourage beginners—why I invest in NSBE/ColorStack inclusion.
stories.task: Push back with mentorship.
stories.action: I run structured intro workshops (DSA basics, git hygiene) so newcomers have an on-ramp.
stories.result: Retention goes up when access is pedagogical, not elitist.

question: What is something 90% of people disagree with you about?

stories.title: Move slow to move fast
stories.situation: Most want to ship Friday fixes; I prefer disciplined rollouts with tests even if boring.
stories.task: Reduce tail risk.
stories.action: MedDiagnose and Hubtel taught me that hotfixes without tests recreate outages; I’d rather ship a minimal, tested patch Monday.
stories.result: Incidents become rare; on-call stays human.

stories.title: Accuracy is not enough in ML
stories.situation: Many focus on leaderboard scores; I care about calibration and workflow integration—often unpopular in hackathons.
stories.task: Clinical reality.
stories.action: I argue for decision-support UX, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and monitoring—not leaderboard bragging.
stories.result: Clinicians engage when the workflow wins are honest; hackathon judges sometimes disagree until they see the pilot metrics.

stories.title: Community > grind culture
stories.situation: I believe sustainable pace beats heroics; some peers glorify all-nighters.
stories.task: Evidence: Dean’s List + shipping.
stories.action: I time-box work, protect sleep during exam weeks, and still ship Geaux/VisualizeMyAlgorithm milestones.
stories.result: Sustainable output beats burnout theater.

question: Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult or bad news to someone.

stories.title: Model limits to stakeholders
stories.situation: I told clinical partners the RF stroke-risk model was ~45% accurate and needed human oversight—bad news for anyone wanting a headline AI win.
stories.task: Transparency preserves trust.
stories.action: I paired the limitation with NIHSS/tPA workflow gains and concrete pilot outcomes—35% faster time-to-treatment.
stories.result: They accepted the system as assistive tooling; the pilot continued with clearer expectations.

stories.title: Sponsor shortfall
stories.situation: NSBE officer team had to tell a partner we missed an attendance promise for an event.
stories.task: Own the miss; present remediation.
stories.action: We offered a smaller targeted workshop with guaranteed RSVPs.
stories.result: Sponsor stayed engaged.

stories.title: Demo failure prep
stories.situation: I told hackathon teammates our live ML endpoint was too risky 6 hours before deadline.
stories.task: Switch to mocks.
stories.action: We recorded outputs, stubbed inference behind a stable API contract, and rehearsed the failure modes.
stories.result: We still won ColorStack Winter 2024 against ~1,000 participants—with a demo that could not flake live.
